sections.summary: Sets out comprehensive sampling procedures: terms and schematic procedures are defined in annexes; ISO recommendations for bulk commodities may be adopted; it mandates preventing contamination/deterioration, sampling each lot separately, determining minimum primary sample numbers from specified tables, preparing bulk and laboratory samples (with mixing or alternative allocation where mixing is inappropriate), keeping detailed sampling records, and packaging/sealing/transporting laboratory samples to protect them in transit.
Requires preventing contamination and deterioration of samples at all stages because they affect analytical results, and that each lot to be checked for compliance must be sampled separately.

Requires the minimum number of primary samples from a lot be determined from Table 1 (or Table 2 for a suspect meat/poultry lot), that each primary sample be taken from a randomly chosen position and contain sufficient material for required laboratory samples, and notes sampling devices are described in ISO/IDF recommendations for specific commodities.

Sets procedures for preparing the bulk sample: for meat and poultry each primary sample is treated as a separate bulk sample; for plant products, eggs and dairy primary samples should be combined and mixed if practicable; where mixing is inappropriate (e.g. large or damage-prone units) units must be randomly allocated to replicate laboratory samples at sampling and the reported result is the mean of valid laboratory results.
For meat and poultry each primary sample is considered a separate bulk sample (i.e. no combining of primaries to form a single bulk sample).

sections.section: 3.3.2 Procedure for plant products, eggs or dairy products (Tables 4 and 5)
sections.summary: For plant products, eggs and dairy, primary samples should, where practicable, be combined and mixed well to form a representative bulk sample.

sections.section: 3.3.3 Alternative procedure where mixing to form the bulk sample is inappropriate or impractical
sections.summary: If mixing or subdivision would damage units or large units cannot be homogenized, units must be randomly allocated to replicate laboratory samples at the time of primary sampling; the numerical result reported should be the mean of valid results from those laboratory samples.

When the bulk sample exceeds the amount needed for analysis it must be reduced to a representative laboratory sample using devices, quartering or other size‑reduction methods (without cutting fresh plant units or whole eggs); replicate laboratory samples may be withdrawn at this stage or as per 3.3.3 and minimum laboratory sample sizes are given in Tables 3–5.

The sampling officer must record lot identity, origin, owner/supplier/carrier, date/place of sampling and any departures from recommended methods; a signed copy of the record must accompany each replicate laboratory sample, a copy retained by the officer and a copy given to the lot owner or representative, and computerized records must be distributed similarly with a verifiable audit trail.

sections.section: 3.6 Packaging and transmission of the laboratory sample
sections.summary: Laboratory samples must be placed in clean, inert, sealed and securely labelled containers with the sampling record attached (barcodes should be accompanied by alphanumeric labels), delivered to the laboratory as soon as practicable and protected against spoilage in transit (e.g. keep fresh samples cool and frozen samples frozen); meat and poultry samples should be frozen before dispatch unless delivery to the laboratory is quick enough to prevent spoilage.

On receipt the laboratory sample must be given a unique identifier with date of receipt and sample size recorded, and the specific part of the commodity to be analysed (the analytical sample) should be separated as soon as practicable, recording weights when residues must be calculated to include unanalyzed parts.

sections.section: 3.8 Preparation and storage of the analytical portion
sections.summary: The analytical sample should be comminuted and mixed as appropriate to allow representative analytical portions, with portion size determined by the analytical method and mixing efficiency and with comminution/mixing methods recorded and not affecting residues; if processing could affect residues use whole units or segments and, where few units are analysed, analyse sufficient replicate portions and withdraw additional portions for replicate and confirmatory analyses, storing portions only under conditions that do not alter residues.

Sets compliance criteria: analytical results must come from one or more laboratory samples fit for analysis and be supported by acceptable quality‑control data (no correction for recovery); where residues exceed an MRL identity must be confirmed and concentration verified by additional analytical portions from the original laboratory sample(s), the Codex MRL applies to the bulk sample and a lot complies if results do not exceed the MRL, while non‑compliance decisions must consider all relevant laboratory results and the analytical accuracy and precision.

sections.section: 4.1 Analytical  results  must  be  derived  from  one  or  more  laboratory  samples  taken  from  the  lot  and received in a fit state for analysis. The results must be supported by acceptable quality control data (e.g. for instrument calibration and pesticide recovery - refer to the Guidelines on good laboratory practice  in  pesticide  residue  analysis  (CAC/GL  40-1993).  Results  should  not be  corrected  for recovery.  Where  a  residue  is  found  to  exceed  an  MRL,  its  identity  should  be  confirmed  and  its concentration must be verified by analysis of one or more additional analytical portions derived from the original laboratory sample(s).
sections.summary: Analytical results must be derived from laboratory sample(s) received in a fit state and supported by acceptable quality‑control data (e.g. instrument calibration and pesticide recovery per CAC/GL 40‑1993); results must not be corrected for recovery, and if a residue exceeds an MRL its identity and concentration must be confirmed by analysis of one or more additional analytical portions from the original laboratory sample(s).

sections.section: 4.2 The Codex MRL applies to the bulk sample.
sections.summary: States that the Codex maximum residue limit (MRL) is to be applied to the bulk sample.

sections.section: 4.3 The lot complies with a Codex MRL where the MRL is not exceeded by the analytical result(s).
sections.summary: Declares that a lot is compliant with a Codex MRL when the analytical result(s) do not exceed that MRL.

sections.section: 4. Poultry meats , type 07, group 036 Note: for enforcement of MRLs for fat soluble pesticides samples must be taken according to section 5 below.
sections.summary: A tabular sampling matrix (truncated here) specifying commodity classifications, examples, nature of primary samples and minimum laboratory sample sizes for animal‑origin commodities (including mammalian meats and fats), with repeated notes that samples for enforcement of fat‑soluble pesticide MRLs must follow the procedures in section 2 and many minimum lab sizes shown as 0.5 kg.

Specifies sampling for large‑sized birds (>2 kg) such as turkey, goose and mature chicken: primary samples are thighs, legs and other dark meat and the minimum laboratory sample is 0.5 kg after removal of skin and bone.

Specifies sampling for medium‑sized birds (500 g–2 kg) such as duckling, guinea fowl and young chicken: collect thighs, legs or other dark meat from at least three birds and provide a minimum laboratory sample of 0.5 kg after removal of skin and bone.
Specifies sampling for small‑sized birds (<500 g) such as quail and pigeon: primary samples are carcasses from at least six birds and the minimum laboratory sample is 0.2 kg of muscle tissue.

sections.section: 4.4 Bird parts fresh/chilled/frozen, retail or wholesale packaged
sections.summary: For bird parts (fresh/chilled/frozen, retail or wholesale packaged) such as legs or quarters, primary samples are packaged units or individual parts and the minimum laboratory sample is 0.5 kg after removal of skin and bone.

sections.section: Class B, primary food commodities of animal origin
sections.summary: A repeated tabular listing (truncated) of Class B primary food commodities of animal origin (mammalian meats, fats, offals, etc.) giving sample types, examples and minimum laboratory sample sizes (commonly 0.5 kg) and noting special sampling for fat‑soluble pesticides.

sections.section: 5. Poultry fats , including carcass fat, type 07, group 037 Note: samples of fat taken as described in 5.1 and 5.2 may be used to determine compliance of the fat, or the whole product, with the corresponding MRLs
sections.summary: Tabulates sampling procedures and minimum laboratory sizes for poultry fats: abdominal fat units from at least three birds (0.5 kg), bird meat parts where visible fat is trimmed (0.5 kg) or whole units/portions where fat cannot be trimmed (up to 2 kg as indicated), and bulk bird fat taken from at least three positions with a minimum laboratory sample of 0.5 kg.

sections.section: 5.1 Birds, at slaughter, whole or part-carcass
sections.summary: Sampling for birds at slaughter (whole or part‑carcass, e.g., chickens, turkeys): collect units of abdominal fat from at least 3 birds, minimum laboratory sample 0.5 kg.
Bird meat parts (e.g., legs, breast): collect visible fat trimmed from unit(s), minimum laboratory sample 0.5 kg.
Bird fat tissue in bulk: take units with a sampling device from at least three positions, minimum laboratory sample 0.5 kg.
Heading for poultry offals (type 07, group 038); sub‑sections (6.1–6.2) provide the specific sampling requirements.

sections.section: 6.1 Edible bird offal, except goose and duck fat liver and similar high value products
sections.summary: Edible bird offal (excluding goose/duck fat liver and similar high‑value products): collect units from at least 6 birds or a cross‑section from a container, minimum laboratory sample 0.2 kg.

sections.section: 6.2 Goose and duck fat liver and similar high value products
sections.summary: Goose and duck fat liver and similar high‑value products: sample one unit from a bird or container, minimum laboratory sample 0.05 kg.

sections.section: Class E, processed foods of animal origin
sections.summary: Class E heading for processed foods of animal origin; not a substantive sampling instruction.

sections.section: 7. Secondary food commodities of animal origin , type 16, group 080 dried meats Derived edible products of animal origin , type 17, group 085 processed animal fats Manufactured food (single ingredient) of animal origin , type 18 Manufactured food (multi-ingredient) of animal origin , type 19
sections.summary: Heading for secondary/derived/processed foods of animal origin (types 16–19); see sub‑sections for the detailed sampling requirements.

sections.section: 7.1 Mammal or bird, comminuted, cooked canned, dried, rendered, or otherwise processed products, including multi-ingredient products
sections.summary: Mammal or bird comminuted/cooked/canned/dried/rendered/processed products including multi‑ingredient items (e.g., ham, sausage, minced meat, pastes): sample packaged units or a representative cross‑section or units (including juices), minimum 0.5 kg or 2 kg if fat content is <5%.

sections.section: Table 4. Plant  products:  description  of  primary  samples  and  minimum  size  of  laboratory samples
sections.summary: Table 4 title for plant products sampling (description of primary samples and minimum laboratory sizes); not a substantive rule by itself.

sections.section: 1. All fresh fruits , type 1, groups 001-008 All fresh vegetables , type 2, groups 009-019, except group 015 (dry pulses)
sections.summary: Heading for all fresh fruits (type 1) and fresh vegetables (type 2, except dry pulses); sub‑sections 1.1–1.3 specify sample unit types and minimum sizes.

sections.section: 1.1 small sized fresh products units generally < 25 g
sections.summary: Small fresh products (<25 g) such as berries, peas, olives: take whole units, packages, or units with a sampling device, minimum laboratory sample 1 kg.

sections.section: 1.2 medium sized fresh products units generally 25-250 g
sections.summary: Medium fresh products (25–250 g) such as apples and oranges: take whole units, minimum laboratory sample 1 kg (at least 10 units).

sections.section: 1.3 large sized fresh products units generally > 250 g
sections.summary: Large fresh products (>250 g) such as cabbages, cucumbers and grape bunches: take whole units, minimum laboratory sample 2 kg (at least 5 units).
Grouping for pulses, cereal grains, tree nuts and oilseeds with indicated minimum samples: pulses/cereal grains/tree nuts — 1 kg; oilseeds — 5 units or 500 g; seeds for beverages/sweets — 500 g.
Oilseeds (type 4, group 023), e.g., peanuts: minimum laboratory sample 5 units or 500 g.

sections.section: Seeds for beverages and sweets , type 4, group 024
sections.summary: Seeds for beverages and sweets (type 4, group 024), e.g., coffee beans: minimum laboratory sample 500 g.

sections.section: 2.1 soya beans rice, wheat except coconuts coconuts
sections.summary: Pulses/cereal grains/tree nuts (e.g., soya beans, rice, wheat, coconuts): minimum laboratory sample 1 kg for each commodity.
Peanuts (oilseeds): minimum laboratory sample 5 units or 500 g.
Fresh herbs (type 5, group 027): take whole units; minimum laboratory sample indicated as 0.5 kg or 0.2 kg depending on the specific herb/item.

Table entries for commodity classes 5.3–5.4 giving sampling requirements: 5.3 Other solid products (e.g., bread flour, apple pomace, dried fruit) — primary samples are packages or whole units or units taken with a sampling device and minimum laboratory sample 0.5 kg; 5.4 Liquid products (e.g., vegetable oils, juices) — packaged units or units taken with a sampling device and minimum laboratory sample 0.5 L or 0.5 kg.

sections.section: Class C, primary animal feed commodities
sections.summary: Table for Class C primary animal feed commodities of plant origin (type 11) specifying sampling: legume animal feeds and other forages/fodders — whole units or units taken with a sampling device, minimum laboratory sample 1 kg (at least 10 units); straw, hay and other dried products — units taken with a sampling device, minimum laboratory sample 0.5 kg (at least 10 units).

Section/table header and rows for Class C primary animal feed commodities of plant origin (type 11) presenting the same sampling requirements as section 45 for legume feeds and for straw/hay (sampling devices, whole units, and minimum laboratory sample sizes).
Row 4.1: Legume animal feeds and other forages/fodders — primary samples are whole units or units taken with a sampling device and minimum laboratory sample 1 kg (at least 10 units).
Row 4.2: Straw, hay and other dried products — primary samples are units taken with a sampling device and minimum laboratory sample 0.5 kg (at least 10 units).

sections.section: Class D, processed foods of plant origin
sections.summary: Table header describing Class D processed foods of plant origin and listing types and examples (secondary food commodities, derived products, single- and multi-ingredient manufactured foods where plant origin predominates, including breads) used to classify items for sampling.

Table for Class D processed plant foods giving sampling requirements across subcategories: 5.1 (high unit value) — packages/units taken with a sampling device, min 0.1 kg; 5.2 (low bulk density solids, e.g., hops, tea) — packaged units or units with sampling device, min 0.2 kg; 5.3 other solid products — packages/whole units or units with sampling device, min 0.5 kg; 5.4 liquids (e.g., vegetable oils, juices) — packaged units or units with sampling device, min 0.5 L or 0.5 kg.
Row 5.1: Products of high unit value — primary samples are packages or units taken with a sampling device and the minimum laboratory sample size is 0.1 kg.
Row 5.2: Solid products of low bulk density (e.g., hops, tea) — primary samples are packaged units or units taken with a sampling device and the minimum laboratory sample size is 0.2 kg.

Annex I provides definitions of sampling-related terms (analytical portion, analytical sample, bulk sample, laboratory sample, lot, primary sample, etc.) with explanatory notes on preparation, representativeness and relationships between primary, bulk and laboratory samples.
Analytical portion: a representative quantity removed from the analytical sample of appropriate size to measure residue concentration, and may be withdrawn using a sampling device.

Analytical sample: the material prepared for analysis from the laboratory sample by separating the portion to be analysed and then mixing/grinding/etc. to allow removal of analytical portions with minimal sampling error, and preparation must reflect the procedure used in setting Codex MRLs (may include parts not normally consumed).

Bulk sample: for products other than meat and poultry, the combined well-mixed aggregate of primary samples from a lot (for meat and poultry the primary sample is equivalent to the bulk sample), with notes that primary samples must provide sufficient material for laboratory samples and that the bulk sample can be considered the conceptual sum of laboratory samples when prepared during collection.

Laboratory sample: the sample sent to or received by the laboratory, being a representative quantity removed from the bulk sample; it may be whole or part of the bulk sample, units should not be cut or broken except where specified, and replicate laboratory samples may be prepared.

Lot: a quantity of food delivered at one time and known or presumed by the sampling officer to have uniform characteristics (origin, producer, variety, packer, packing, markings, consignor, etc.), with definitions of suspect versus non-suspect lots and notes on consignments, multiple lots and lot boundaries.

Primary sample: one or more units taken from one position in a lot, with notes that the sampling position should preferably be chosen randomly or from an accessible random position, the number of units is determined by laboratory sample needs, contributions to bulk samples should be similar for plant/egg/dairy products, units may be randomly allocated to replicate laboratory samples in certain cases, sampling position can be a point in time during loading/unloading, and units should not be cut or broken except where subdivision is specified.

Sample: one or more units selected from a population or a portion taken from a larger quantity, and for these recommendations a representative sample is intended to represent the lot/bulk sample/animal with respect to pesticide residue content (not necessarily other attributes).
Sampling: the procedure used to draw and constitute a sample.

Sampling device: tools such as scoops, dippers, borers, knives or spears used to remove units from bulk material or packages (or from large meat/poultry units), and tools such as riffle boxes used to prepare laboratory samples from bulk samples or analytical portions, with reference to ISO and IDF standards for specific devices.

sections.section: Notes. (a) Specific sampling devices are described by ISO 1,2,3  and IDF 4  standards.
sections.summary: Note stating that specific sampling devices are described by ISO and IDF standards.

Defines a sampling officer as a person trained (and where required authorized) to take samples and makes them responsible for all procedures through preparation, packing, shipping and documentation of laboratory samples, with an obligation to adhere to specified sampling procedures and collaborate with the laboratory.
Defines sample size as the number of units or quantity of material that constitutes the sample.
Defines a unit as the smallest discrete portion in a lot to be withdrawn to form the whole or part of a primary sample, with a note that units should be identified as specified.
